[F15] ENGINEERING IN GENERAL -- F15 covers general engineering concepts and practices that apply across the mechanical engineering spectrum, including fundamental design principles, analysis methods, and common technical solutions not limited to a specific device or subsystem. It serves as a catch-all for inventions and innovations that address overall engineering problems rather than particular mechanical components.
[F15B] SYSTEMS ACTING BY MEANS OF FLUIDS IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems transferring mechanical energy by means of a fluid under pressure using the principles of fluid statics or hydrostatics, i.e. hydraulic or pneumatic systems  **Glossary:** - Pneumatic: using air or an inert gas (except steam) as a pressure medium. - Hydraulic: using a liquid as the pressure medium. - Telemotor: system or device in which a substantially constant amount of fluid is trapped between an input member and an output member to act as a fluid link - Servomotor: fluid-pressure actuator, e.g. a piston and a cylinder, directly controlled by a valve or other device (e.g. pump) which is responsive to operation of an initial controlling member (e.g. joystick). The initial controlling member may be adjacent to the servomotor or at a distance and may be, e.g. a hand lever.
[F15B 1/00] Installations or systems with accumulators -- **Glossary:** - Accumulator: vessel for storing pressurised liquid (e.g. using a liquid and a gas chamber separated by a membrane or piston or using an elastic housing)
[F15B 1/02] Installations or systems with accumulators -- **Glossary:** - Accumulator: vessel for storing pressurised liquid (e.g. using a liquid and a gas chamber separated by a membrane or piston or using an elastic housing)
[F15B 1/021] used for damping -- This subclass covers installations or systems that use fluid accumulators primarily for damping, such as hydraulic shock absorbers, vibration isolators, or other fluid-based energy-dissipating devices. It includes designs where the accumulator absorbs kinetic energy and releases it gradually to reduce oscillations and control motion.
[F15B 1/022] used as an emergency power source, e.g. in case of pump failure -- A system or installation that incorporates accumulators to supply emergency power, for example when a pump fails, is covered by this subclass. It concerns fluid-based mechanisms that temporarily store energy to keep the equipment operating during a power loss.
[F15B 1/024] used as a supplementary power source, e.g. to store energy in idle periods to balance pump load -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also for recuperation of hydraulic energy (as e.g. used in hydraulic excavators)
[F15B 1/025] used for thermal compensation, e.g. to collect expanded fluid and to return it to the system as the system fluid cools down -- F15B 1/025 covers devices that provide thermal compensation in fluid installations by collecting fluid that expands on heating and returning it to the system as it cools. Such mechanisms help maintain pressure and volume stability in pipelines, hydraulic circuits or other fluid-based systems.
[F15B 1/027] having accumulator charging devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. valves controlling flow of hydraulic fluid to and from the liquid chamber of an accumulator.
[F15B 1/0275] with two or more pilot valves, e.g. for independent setting of the cut-in and cut-out pressures -- The class covers fluid-accumulator installations that employ two or more pilot valves so that the cut-in pressure for charging and the cut-out pressure for discharging can be set independently, allowing separate control of the accumulator's start and stop thresholds in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 1/033] with electrical control means -- This subclass covers installations or systems that include accumulators whose operation is managed by electrical control devices such as sensors, switches, controllers or electronically actuated valves. It pertains to fluid-acting mechanical engineering systems where electrical means regulate the charging, discharging or pressure maintenance of the accumulator.
[F15B 1/04] Accumulators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Vessel for storing pressurised liquid (e.g. using a liquid and a gas chamber separated by a membrane or piston or using an elastic housing).
[F15B 1/045] Dead weight accumulators -- Dead-weight accumulators are hydraulic devices that store energy by using a column of fluid acting on a weighted piston or plate, allowing pressure to be maintained or released as the fluid moves. They smooth pressure fluctuations, provide standby power, or absorb shocks in fluid-power systems, relying on gravity for the acting force.
[F15B 1/08] using a gas cushion -- The code F15B 1/08 refers to installations or systems that employ a gas cushion as the energy-storage element. It covers fluid-based mechanical devices where a compressible gas accumulates to absorb shocks, provide spring action, or assist motion, and is situated under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-system technology.
[F15B 1/083] the accumulator having a fusible plug -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accumulators with a safety plugs that melt at a certain temperature for relieving the pressure.
[F15B 1/086] the gas cushion being entirely enclosed by the separating means, e.g. foam or gas-filled balls -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accumulators using bladders -> F15B1/165
[F15B 1/10] with flexible separating means -- F15B 1/10 refers to accumulators or similar fluid-power installations that employ flexible separating means, such as membranes, diaphragms or bellows, to divide the working fluid from a gas charge. The flexible element provides a seal while allowing volume change as the accumulator stores or releases energy, and the code is used for hydraulic and pneumatic systems that incorporate this type of separator.
[F15B 11/00] Servomotor systems without provision for follow-up action; -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Servomotor systems without provision for follow-up action and circuits therefor  **Glossary:** - Servomotor: fluid-pressure acutator, e.g. a piston and a cylinder, directly controlled by a valve or other device (e.g. pump) which is responsive to operation of an initial controlling member (e.g. joystick). The initial controlling member may be adjacent to the servomotor or at a distance and may be, e.g. a hand lever.
[F15B 11/003] Systems with load-holding valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This class comprises releasable check valves and proportional braking valves.
[F15B 11/006] Hydraulic "Wheatstone bridge" circuits, i.e. with four nodes, P-A-T-B, and on-off or proportional valves in each link -- F15B 11/006 covers hydraulic Wheatstone bridge circuits, which use four interconnected nodes (P-A-T-B) and incorporate on-off or proportional valves in each branch to sense and control fluid pressure in servomotor systems lacking follow-up action.
[F15B 11/02] Systems essentially incorporating special features for controlling the speed or actuating force of an output member -- F15B 11/02 refers to servomotor systems that do not provide a follow-up action and incorporate special features for controlling the speed or actuating force of the output. These fluid-based mechanisms belong to the mechanical-engineering branch of systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/022] in which a rapid approach stroke is followed by a slower, high-force working stroke  F15B11/0325  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - fluid-pressure converters increasing the working force after an approach stroke -> F15B11/0325
[F15B 11/024] by means of differential connection of the servomotor lines, e.g. regenerative circuits -- F15B 11/024 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up mechanism and use a differential connection of the motor lines, such as regenerative circuits. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-actuated systems.
[F15B 11/028] for controlling the actuating force -- This CPC group refers to servo-motor systems that do not incorporate a feedback or follow-up action and are used to regulate the force applied by the actuator, placed within the broader classes of mechanical engineering, general engineering and fluid-power systems.
[F15B 1/103] the separating means being bellows -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  Fig. from US2004244857
[F15B 11/032] by means of fluid-pressure converters -- This subclass covers servomotor arrangements that operate without any feedback or follow-up mechanism and transmit motion through hydraulic or other fluid-pressure converters. It is situated within fluid-based system engineering under the broader mechanical engineering field.
[F15B 11/0325] the fluid-pressure converter increasing the working force after an approach stroke -- This CPC entry covers servomotor systems lacking follow-up action, specifically the fluid-pressure converter that boosts the output force after the piston's approach stroke. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-acting systems.
[F15B 11/036] by means of servomotors having a plurality of working chambers -- The class F15B 11/036 pertains to fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that employ several working chambers to produce motion, but do not include any follow-up or feedback action. It covers mechanical systems in which the plurality of chambers provides the driving force directly, without additional control loops, and is used for precise positioning or speed regulation in hydraulic or pneumatic applications.
[F15B 11/0365] Tandem constructions -- The classification F15B 11/0365 covers tandem constructions employed in servomotor systems that do not include any follow-up action. It refers to arrangements where two or more motor units are linked in series to deliver combined motion or force, often in fluid-driven or hydraulic actuation contexts. These constructions are used in mechanical engineering applications that operate without feedback or control loops.
[F15B 11/04] for controlling the speed -- F15B 11/04 covers speed control mechanisms for servomotor systems that operate without any follow-up action, typically using hydraulic or pneumatic power. The classification falls under mechanical engineering and applies to fluid-driven actuator arrangements where the motor speed is regulated by valves, throttles or other speed-adjusting devices.
[F15B 11/0406] during starting or stopping  F15B11/048  takes precedence
[F15B 11/0413] in one direction only, with no control in the reverse direction, e.g. check valve in parallel with a throttle valve -- The entry refers to servomotor systems that permit motion only in a single direction and provide no control for reverse motion, such as a check valve placed in parallel with a throttle valve. This configuration ensures one-way fluid actuation without follow-up action in the opposite direction.
[F15B 11/042] by means in the feed line -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Servomotor systems for controlling the speed of an output member, the speed depending on the position of the working member -> F15B11/046 - Servomotor systems specially adapted to maintain constant speed force of an output member -> F15B11/05
[F15B 11/0423] by controlling pump output or bypass, other than to maintain constant speed -- This CPC subclass deals with fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that lack a feedback or follow-up mechanism and regulate motion by varying the pump's output or by diverting flow, rather than by keeping the motor speed constant. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/0426] by controlling the number of pumps or parallel valves switched on -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. systems controlling the speed by means of so-called digital valves or by means of systems using pulse code modulation (PCM)
[F15B 11/044] by means in the return line -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Servomotor systems for controlling the speed of an output member, the speed depending on the position of the working member -> F15B11/046 - Servomotor systems specially adapted to maintain constant speed force of an output member -> F15B11/05
[F15B 11/0445] with counterbalance valves, e.g. to prevent overrunning or for braking -- This subclass covers fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that lack a feedback or follow-up mechanism but incorporate counterbalance valves to limit overspeed, prevent overrunning, or provide a braking function. Such valves are used to balance load forces and improve control in hydraulic or pneumatic actuation systems.
[F15B 11/046] depending on the position of the working member -- F15B 11/046 concerns servomotor systems that operate without follow-up action and whose control depends on the position of the working member. It includes arrangements where the actuator's output is varied or regulated according to the location of the moving part, typically using positional sensing rather than continuous feedback.
[F15B 11/048] with deceleration control -- F15B 11/048 covers fluid-power servomotor arrangements that lack any feedback or follow-up device yet incorporate a mechanism to control deceleration, such as braking or throttling, when the motor slows down. It is nested under mechanical engineering, general engineering and fluid-acting system categories.
[F15B 11/05] specially adapted to maintain constant speed, e.g. pressure-compensated, load-responsive -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - maintaining constant speed with sensing of servomotor demand and load, for two or more servomotors -> F15B11/161
[F15B 11/055] by adjusting the pump output or bypass -- This CPC subclass deals with servomotor systems that do not include a follow-up mechanism and control motion by varying the pump output or by using a bypass. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 1/106] characterised by the way housing components are assembled -- F15B 1/106 covers fluid-power installations that include accumulators and are distinguished by the specific method used to assemble the housing components of the system. The classification is nested under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, systems acting by means of fluids in general, and installations or systems with accumulators.
[F15B 11/06] involving features specific to the use of a compressible medium, e.g. air, steam -- F15B 11/06 covers servomotor systems that operate using a compressible medium such as air or steam, without follow-up action. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-based systems.
[F15B 11/064] with devices for saving the compressible medium -- The classification covers servomotor arrangements that lack an internal follow-up (feedback) function but include components such as accumulators, gas springs or other storage devices that retain the compressed fluid used to drive the motor, enabling the system to conserve or reuse the pressurised medium for improved efficiency and response.
[F15B 11/068] with valves for gradually putting pneumatic systems under pressure -- F15B 11/068 pertains to servomotor configurations that use valves to incrementally increase the pressure in pneumatic systems, operating without any follow-up or feedback control for motion or position.
[F15B 11/072] Combined pneumatic-hydraulic systems -- The F15B 11/072 subclass covers combined pneumatic-hydraulic servomotor arrangements that generate motion using both air and oil but do not include any follow-up or feedback mechanism. These systems are used where fluid-power actuation is required without closed-loop control, falling under mechanical engineering and fluid-system categories.
[F15B 11/0725] with the driving energy being derived from a pneumatic system, a subsequent hydraulic system displacing or controlling the output element -- F15B 11/0725 defines servomotor arrangements that generate motion from a pneumatic source and then transmit that energy to a hydraulic subsystem which moves or regulates the output element, without any feedback mechanism to adjust the motion in real time.
[F15B 11/076] with pneumatic drive or displacement and speed control or stopping by hydraulic braking -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that use a pneumatic actuator for movement and employ hydraulic braking to control speed or stop motion, without any feedback or follow-up mechanism. It falls under fluid-powered mechanical engineering categories in the CPC.
[F15B 11/08] with only one servomotor -- This subclass covers fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that employ a single servomotor and lack any mechanism for automatic follow-up or corrective action. It applies to systems where the motor directly controls a fluid actuator without feedback loops.
[F15B 11/10] in which the servomotor position is a function of the pressure -- The classification F15B 11/10 defines fluid-actuated servomotor systems where the motor's position is determined solely by the applied pressure, lacking any separate feedback or follow-up control. It falls under mechanical engineering topics dealing with general fluid-driven systems.
[F15B 11/12] providing distinct intermediate positions -- The CPC subclass F15B 11/12 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up action and specifies how to provide distinct intermediate positions within the motor's movement. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/121] providing distinct intermediate positions  F15B11/13  takes precedence -- F15B 11/121 refers to servomotor systems that do not include a follow-up action mechanism but are capable of holding distinct intermediate positions, allowing the motor to stop at several predefined points instead of continuously tracking a signal. It is a narrower subclass of F15B 11/13, which covers servomotors without follow-up action in general.
[F15B 11/122] by means of actuators with multiple stops -- F15B 11/122 pertains to fluid-driven servomotor arrangements that lack continuous feedback control and achieve positioning by using actuators equipped with several preset stops. The design provides discrete movement steps rather than proportional or follow-up action.
[F15B 11/123] by means of actuators with fluid-operated stops -- F15B 11/123 pertains to servomotor systems that lack follow-up action and employ actuators equipped with fluid-operated stops. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-powered systems, and addresses the use of fluid-controlled stopping mechanisms to position or limit motion.
[F15B 11/125] by means of digital actuators, i.e. actuators in which the total stroke is the sum of individual strokes -- F15B 11/125 covers servomotor systems that lack follow-up action and use digital actuators, where the overall displacement results from a series of discrete strokes added together. These systems belong to the fluid-powered mechanical engineering group, specifically engineering in general and fluid-based system categories.
[F15B 11/126] by means of actuators of the standard type with special circuit controlling means  F15B11/125  takes precedence -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that operate without a follow-up action, where motion is produced by standard-type actuators directed by special control circuits. It is a refinement of F15B11/125 within fluid-powered system engineering.
[F15B 11/127] with step-by-step action -- The CPC subclass F15B 11/127 covers servomotor systems that operate with step-by-step action but lack any feedback or follow-up mechanism. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general, describing fluid-driven servomotors that move sequentially without closed-loop control.
[F15B 11/128] by means of actuators of the standard type with special circuit controlling means -- The subclass F15B 11/128 defines servomotor systems that operate without a feedback or follow-up mechanism and employ standard-type actuators together with a special control circuit. It covers hydraulic or pneumatic servos whose motion is directed solely by the circuit logic rather than by sensor-based correction, fitting within the broader category of fluid-based mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 11/13] using chambers of predetermined volume -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that use chambers of a fixed predetermined volume to produce motion, without any feedback or follow-up control. The chambers are sized to deliver a set displacement or force when pressurised, enabling precise but open-loop operation.
[F15B 11/15] with special provision for automatic return -- F15B 11/15 refers to fluid-driven servomotor systems that incorporate a built-in mechanism to automatically return the output element to a reference position after actuation, eliminating the need for external follow-up control.
[F15B 11/16] with two or more servomotors -- F15B 11/16 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up action mechanism and employ two or more servomotors, typically used to control fluid-driven mechanisms such as valves or pumps. The class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/161] with sensing of servomotor demand or load -- The entry refers to servomotor systems that do not incorporate automatic follow-up control but are equipped with a sensor that measures the motor's demand or load. It applies to mechanisms where the sensed load is used for monitoring or occasional adjustment rather than continuous feedback regulation.
[F15B 11/162] for giving priority to particular servomotors or users
[F15B 11/163] for sharing the pump output equally amongst users or groups of users, e.g. using anti-saturation, pressure compensation -- F15B 11/163 classifies hydraulic servomotor systems that operate without feedback or follow-up control and provide equal flow distribution to several users or user groups, employing techniques such as anti-saturation or pressure compensation to balance pump output across the loads.
[F15B 11/165] for adjusting the pump output or bypass in response to demand -- F15B 11/165 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up mechanism and are used to modify a pump's output or to operate a bypass line in line with changing demand, typically by adjusting flow or pressure automatically as the required performance varies. This falls under fluid-based mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 11/166] Controlling a pilot pressure in response to the load, i.e. supply to at least one user is regulated by adjusting either the system pilot pressure or one or more of the individual pilot command pressures -- This CPC subclass F15B 11/166 covers servomotor hydraulic systems that regulate a pilot pressure in response to load changes, controlling the supply to a user by varying either the overall system pilot pressure or the individual pilot command pressures. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-powered systems.
[F15B 11/167] using pilot pressure to sense the demand -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up mechanism and employ pilot pressure as a sensing signal to detect the demanded motion or force. The pilot-pressure feedback is used solely to sense demand, not to provide continuous corrective action.
[F15B 11/168] with an isolator valve (duplicating valve), i.e. at least one load sense [LS] pressure is derived from a work port load sense pressure but is not a work port pressure itself -- It covers hydraulic servomotor systems that lack follow-up control and incorporate an isolator or duplicating valve that provides a load-sense pressure derived from a work-port pressure, used for control but not as the actual work pressure.
[F15B 11/17] using two or more pumps -- F15B 11/17 concerns servomotor fluid-power arrangements that employ two or more pumps to supply the required pressure or flow, but do not include any follow-up or feedback action. The subclass addresses mechanical systems where multiple pumps are coordinated to drive a servomotor without closed-loop control.
[F15B 11/18] used in combination for obtaining stepwise operation of a single controlled member -- F15B 11/18 covers servomotor arrangements that lack feedback for continuous control and are combined to produce incremental motion of a single actuator. The classification is applied to systems that achieve stepwise positioning by linking multiple servomotors or using gear mechanisms to move one controlled member in discrete steps.
[F15B 11/183] Linear stepwise operation -- This class covers servomotor arrangements that produce linear motion in discrete steps without any feedback or automatic follow-up control. The devices are used in systems where motion is commanded stepwise, such as positioning or actuation mechanisms, and rely on fluid-driven actuation rather than continuous regulation. It falls under mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-based system categories.
[F15B 11/186] Rotary stepwise operation -- F15B 11/186 covers rotary stepwise operation in servomotor systems that lack any follow-up action, meaning the motor moves in discrete angular increments without continuous control feedback. It falls under fluid-driven mechanical engineering for general fluid-actuated systems.
[F15B 1/12] attached at their periphery -- The subgroup addresses fluid-power installations in which one or more accumulators are mounted on the outer part of the system rather than inside the main housing, providing peripheral pressure regulation, energy storage, or shock-absorption functions.
[F15B 11/20] controlling several interacting or sequentially-operating members -- F15B 11/20 relates to control of multiple interacting or sequentially-operating members within servomotor systems that do not provide follow-up action. The subclass covers fluid-powered arrangements that coordinate the movement of several components to achieve a target function, such as hydraulic or pneumatic devices where the control logic synchronises successive or coupled actions.
[F15B 11/205] the position of the actuator controlling the fluid flow to the subsequent actuator -- F15B 11/205 concerns the spatial arrangement or setting of an actuator that governs the fluid flow to a downstream actuator in fluid-driven servomotor systems that do not include a follow-up or feedback mechanism. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically in the category of systems operating with fluids, and addresses how the controlling actuator is positioned to influence subsequent motion.
[F15B 11/22] Synchronisation of the movement of two or more servomotors -- This CPC subclass covers the coordination of two or more servomotors in fluid-driven mechanical systems, describing methods and devices that synchronize their motion without provision for follow-up action.
[F15B 1/125] characterised by the attachment means  F15B1/14  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F15B 1/125 covers fluid-power installations or systems that include accumulators and are distinguished by the specific means used to attach those accumulators to the system, such as brackets, couplings or mounting structures. This subclass falls under the broader group for systems acting by means of fluids and inherits the general features of F15B 1/14, which must be read first.
[F15B 1/14] by means of a rigid annular supporting member -- F15B 1/14 classifies fluid-driven installations or systems with accumulators that incorporate a rigid annular supporting member, such as a circumferential ring or sleeve, to stabilize or guide moving components within the hydraulic or pneumatic circuit. The feature is used to enhance alignment or bearing capacity of the accumulator assembly.
[F15B 1/16] in the form of a tube -- F15B 1/16 classifies accumulator installations that are built as tubes, such as pipe-type accumulators used to store pressurised fluid. The code applies to fluid-storage systems where the storage element is a tubular container rather than a separate vessel or bladder.
[F15B 1/165] in the form of a bladder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accumulators using bladders, i.e. essentially cylindrical flexible separating means having a first open end portion which is normally rounded and a second end portion with a fluid inlet for separating a hydraulic fluid and a gas.  Fig. from US2006042707
[F15B 1/18] Anti-extrusion means -- F15B 1/18 covers anti-extrusion devices used in fluid systems with accumulators to prevent material from escaping the containment area. These means are employed in mechanical-engineering installations that store fluid pressure, such as hydraulic accumulators, and typically include seals, barriers or locking mechanisms.
[F15B 1/20] fixed to the separating means -- F15B 1/20 covers installations or systems that include accumulators in which a component is permanently attached to the separating element that divides the high-pressure and low-pressure chambers, such as a fixed piston, valve or membrane that remains stationary relative to the accumulator body.
[F15B 1/22] Liquid port constructions -- F15B 1/22 covers the design and arrangement of liquid ports used to introduce or discharge fluid in installations that contain accumulators, such as hydraulic or pneumatic storage tanks. It includes the shape, size, positioning and sealing features of inlet, outlet or vent connections that control the flow of liquid to and from the accumulator.
[F15B 1/24] with rigid separating means, e.g. pistons -- The subclass covers fluid-powered installations that incorporate accumulators using rigid separating elements such as pistons to separate the working fluid from a pressurised gas or spring. It pertains to hydraulic or pneumatic systems where the piston-type separator provides a defined, non-flexible barrier to store or release energy.
[F15B 1/26] Supply reservoir or sump assemblies -- The entry F15B 1/26 pertains to supply reservoir or sump assemblies used in fluid-powered installations, describing tanks, sumps and accumulator units that store and provide fluid to hydraulic or pneumatic systems, together with their associated piping and mounting arrangements.
[F15B 1/265] with pressurised main reservoir -- F15B 1/265 covers installations or systems that incorporate accumulators with a pressurised main reservoir, used to store fluid under pressure in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits for mechanical-engineering applications.
[F15B 13/00] Details of servomotor systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Components of hydraulic or pneumatic circuits such as valves and flow dividers.
[F15B 13/01] Locking-valves or other detent devices -- The CPC subclass F15B 13/01 covers locking-valves and other detent mechanisms used in servomotor systems that control fluid flow. It includes devices that retain a valve in a selected position without continuous power, such as mechanical catches or magnetic latches integrated into hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 13/015] using an enclosed pilot flow valve -- F15B 13/015 covers the use of an enclosed pilot flow valve within servomotor systems, detailing how the valve regulates fluid flow to control actuator motion. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically systems acting by means of fluids in general, and addresses design features that isolate the pilot valve from the main fluid circuit.
[F15B 13/02] Fluid distribution or supply devices characterised by their adaptation to the control of servomotors -- F15B 13/02 denotes fluid-distribution or supply devices specially adapted for controlling servomotors, such as hydraulic or pneumatic circuits that regulate flow to drive precision servomotor systems. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 13/021] Valves for interconnecting the fluid chambers of an actuator -- F15B 13/021 defines valves that interconnect the fluid chambers of an actuator, used in hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems to direct fluid between chambers and control motion. Such valves may be built into single or multiple chambers and are essential for bidirectional actuation and position regulation.
[F15B 13/022] Flow-dividers; Priority valves -- F15B 13/022 relates to flow-dividing devices and priority valves that control the distribution of fluid in hydraulic servomotor systems. These components direct fluid to multiple actuators or give precedence to a particular circuit, improving response and stability in fluid-powered mechanisms.
[F15B 13/023] Excess flow valves, e.g. for locking cylinders in case of hose burst -- F15B 13/023 concerns excess-flow valves that automatically limit fluid flow, such as devices that lock hydraulic cylinders when a hose bursts. These components are part of servomotor systems and fall under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-based systems. They protect equipment by detecting abnormal pressure drops and shutting off flow to prevent damage.
[F15B 13/024] Pressure relief valves -- Pressure relief valves are safety components that automatically open to discharge excess fluid pressure, preventing damage to hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems. They are designed to sense a preset pressure threshold and vent flow when that limit is exceeded, thereby protecting actuators and associated equipment from overload.
[F15B 13/025] Pressure reducing valves -- F15B 13/025 covers pressure reducing valves, which are components of servomotor systems that lower fluid pressure to a desired value, used in hydraulic circuits to control flow and protect downstream equipment.
[F15B 13/026] Pressure compensating valves -- F15B 13/026 covers pressure compensating valves used in hydraulic servomotor systems to automatically adjust flow and keep a set downstream pressure despite load changes. These devices typically employ springs or diaphragms that modulate the valve opening, ensuring stable actuator performance.
[F15B 13/027] Check valves -- F15B 13/027 designates check valves - one-way flow devices that automatically close to prevent backflow - as they are employed in fluid-driven servomotor systems. The code falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids. It covers the structure, operation and integration of check valves in such hydraulic or pneumatic actuator arrangements.
[F15B 13/028] Shuttle valves -- Shuttle valves are fluid-control components that connect a single output to one of two input lines, automatically passing the higher-pressure source while preventing backflow from the lower-pressure side. They are employed in servomotor and hydraulic circuits to switch power sources or protect the system from unwanted pressure changes.
[F15B 13/029] Counterbalance valves -- Counterbalance valves are hydraulic or pneumatic devices that regulate pressure to maintain a constant load or to halt motion when the load exceeds a set limit, providing safety and precise control in servomotor actuator systems. They fall under CPC code F15B 13/029, covering details of servomotor systems in fluid-acting mechanisms.
[F15B 13/04] for use with a single servomotor -- F15B 13/04 covers servomotor systems designed to operate with a single servomotor, including the mechanical arrangement, control linkages and power transmission that enable precise motion control in fluid-based engineering applications. It is used for devices where one motor provides the complete actuation function.
[F15B 13/0401] Valve members; Fluid interconnections therefor -- F15B 13/0401 covers valve elements and the fluid-flow connections that link them, specifically in servomotor systems used for precise motion control. It includes the design of valve bodies, ports, actuating mechanisms and associated piping that direct hydraulic or pneumatic fluids to achieve controlled movement.
[F15B 13/0402] for linearly sliding valves, e.g. spool valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  Fig. from WO2008015752
[F15B 13/0403] a secondary valve member sliding within the main spool, e.g. for regeneration flow  F15B13/0418  takes precedence -- F15B 13/0403 covers details of servomotor systems in fluid-actuated mechanisms, specifically a secondary valve element that slides within the main spool to provide regeneration flow. This sub-group is superseded by the more specific F15B 13/0418, which refines the arrangement and operation of the secondary valve member.
[F15B 13/0405] for seat valves, i.e. poppet valves -- The CPC subclass F15B 13/0405 covers servomotor-driven seat or poppet valves, describing mechanisms where a servo actuator positions the valve seat to control fluid flow. It pertains to hydraulic or pneumatic systems in which the valve is actuated by a servo motor for precise flow regulation.
[F15B 13/0406] for rotary valves -- F15B 13/0406 covers rotary valves used in servomotor systems that control fluid flow. It falls within the mechanical engineering field, specifically systems acting by means of fluids, and relates to detailed aspects of servomotor operation. The classification addresses design, function, and integration of rotary valve mechanisms in fluid-powered actuation.

[F15D] FLUID DYNAMICS, i.e. METHODS OR MEANS FOR INFLUENCING THE FLOW OF GASES OR LIQUIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means and methods not provided for in other classes, for influencing the flow of fluids relative to constraining surfaces and after leaving these surfaces, e.g. producing or removing turbulence, deflecting jets, guiding flow through bends in conduits, affecting distribution of fluid in a conduit, reducing fluid friction.

[F16] ENGINEERING ELEMENTS AND UNITS
[F16B] DEVICES FOR FASTENING OR SECURING CONSTRUCTIONAL ELEMENTS OR MACHINE PARTS TOGETHER, e.g. NAILS, BOLTS, CIRCLIPS, CLAMPS, CLIPS OR WEDGES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening elements for constructional parts per se, such as: clamps, clips, dowels, nails, staples, bolts without screw thread, pins, rivets, circlips, self-cutting screws, screws and bolts with thread, nuts, lock-nuts, threaded inserts, washers, hooks, suction cups  The connection of constructional elements such as key-type connections, shrinkage connections, joining of sheets and plates, connections of rods or tubes mutually or to flat surfaces at an angle, connections of constructional elements by sticking or pressing them together, joining of furniture, connections of constructional elements by a part of one element entering a hole in the other  The fastening elements or connection of elements in this group can be employed in multiple technical application fields. However, the focus is on the fastening element or the connection of elements itself.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Couplings transmitting rotation -> F16D  **Glossary:** - Connections using screw-thread elements: the connection is established by the screw-thread element - A screw acts on another element in order to establish a clamped connection: a connection using clamps

[F16B 13/00] Dowels or other devices fastened in walls or the like by inserting them in holes made therein for that purpose -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dowels made e.g. of wood, metal or plastic. Dowels fixed by a settable material.  **Glossary:** - Dowel: Solid cylindrical rod, usually made of wood, plastic or metal that is usually inserted into a blind hole.

[F16B 9/00] Connections of rods or tubular parts to flat surfaces at an angle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-torque transmitting connections of rods or tubular parts to flat surfaces at an angle, i.e. the rods or tubular parts are non-parallel with respect to the flat surfaces. The rods or tubular parts can be connected perpendicularly to the flat surfaces. The connections can involve an intermediate member.  The rods or tubular parts and the flat surfaces are constructional elements or machine parts.  The rods or tubular parts are elongated elements, of any cross-section, that do not convey fluids. Rods or tubular elements may be flexible, but not flaccid, e.g. a cord is not a rod or tubular element in the sense of F16B9/00 .  Flat surfaces are surfaces that are planar at least locally around the connection location.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections of rods to flat surfaces whereby a part of or on one member enters a hole in the other member and involving plastic deformation -> F16B17/006
[F16B 9/01] Welded or bonded connections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/02] Detachable connections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Connections that can be detached without breaking any part.  Detachable and releasable are synonyms.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections by way of an intermediate member -> F16B9/05 - Connections involving plastic or elastic deformation -> F16B9/07 - Connections where rods and flat surfaces interengage by projections and mating sockets -> F16B9/09
[F16B 9/05] by way of an intermediate member
[F16B 9/052] the intermediate member having a radial flange secured to the flat surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/054] the intermediate member being threaded -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/056] the intermediate member extending through the flat surface; the rod or tubular part extending through the flat surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/058] the intermediate member being secured to the rod by transverse fasteners -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/07] involving plastic or elastic deformation when assembling  involving plastic deformation with a part of or on one member entering a hole in the other  F16B17/006 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections with a part of or on one member entering a hole in the other and involving plastic deformation -> F16B17/006  **Glossary:** - Elastic deformation: The parts recover their initial shape after deformation - Plastic deformation: The parts do not recover their initial shape after deformation
[F16B 9/09] rods and flat surfaces interengaging by projections and mating sockets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16C] SHAFTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The elements of Subclass F16C are various general mechanical engineering elements or units which have in common that they all relate to:  In general Subclass F16C it encompasses:  The elongated elements to transmit linear, rotary or combined movement, e.g. push-pull type movement, comprise:  The supporting elements allowing relative rotary, linear or combined motion comprise:  Since the individual mechanical engineering elements of Subclass F16C are not a coherent group they will be defined individually in more detail below.  General distribution of subject-matter in Subclass F16C (index):  Flexible Mechanical elements for transmitting movement / force F16C1/00 :  Support elements allowing relative displacement between parts:  Bearings  Bearings with sliding or rolling contact:  For parts which both rotate and move linearly F16C31/00  Other bearings ( F16C32/00 )  Details or accessories of bearings:  Bearings are general mechanical engineering elements which support or guide and are adapted to position one part moving with respect to another part of an arrangement, i.e. to allow constrained relative motion between the two parts. One of the parts supports the load imposed thereon by the other part. The bearing is intended to reduce friction between the two relatively moving parts, which are typically larger than the bearing itself.  It is noted that apart from bearings many other objects, such as vehicle wheels, conveyor rollers etc., also meet the above criteria. These objects are classified elsewhere.  Further it is noted here that in general the expression "bearing" is also used for supports between parts which are (quasi) stationary.  Due to the lack of relative movement such bearing are not bearings in the sense of this section of Subclass F16C .  Apart from the bearings per se the section "bearings" also covers:  Bearings are distinguished and classified in the respective groups and groups according to  Movement between the two parts:  Principle of operation, i.e. the means to reduce friction.  Sliding contact, e.g. plain bearings (bushings, journal bearings, sleeve bearings) including:  Loads:  Combination of principles can be used within a bearing or bearing arrangement, e.g. to support different load directions or to take account of various operating conditions, e.g. speed.  **Glossary:** - rotary engineering element: any engineering element, other than a rotating element used in gearing, clutches, couplings or brakes, which rotates so far as its features are affected only by the fact that it rotates. - bearing: a component of a machine or mechanism that is positioned between two relatively movable elements of the machine or mechanism and that has a surface formed for sliding or rolling contact with these elements when they move relative to each other for the sole function of reducing the friction that would otherwise result from their relative movement. - connecting-rod or link pivoted at both ends: a reciprocating or oscillating elongated member of a machine or mechanism that is intended to be pivotally connected to and positioned between two relatively movable elements of the machine or mechanism for the purpose of transmitting force or conveying motion between the elements when it reciprocates or oscillates. - axle: an elongated element to support a rotating part, e.g. a wheel or roll. loads are bending moments and radial load. It may be stationary or rotating with the part fixed thereto. - shaft: an elongated element, normally a rod of circular cross-section, that rotates about its longitudinal axis to transmit torque, e.g. by conveying motion from a gear wheel supported by it to another part of a machine or mechanism. - crankshaft: a shaft with one or more cranks for turning reciprocating or oscillating motion into rotary motion. - flexible shaft: an elongated element, that is bendable along its length and rotates about its longitudinal axis to transmit torque or convey rotary motion. - Bowden cable: a control cable consisting of an elongated member enclosed within a housing bendable along its length and shiftable along its longitudinal axis relative to its housing to transmit motion or force. - roll, drum, disc: an element of a machine or mechanism in the form of a roller that has a generally curved surface that contacts work or (linear) moving part and revolves about its axis when the work shifts relative to it.
[F16C 1/00] Flexible shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  There are two distinct groups of elements which, apart from being elongated and flexible, have a different function:  The shaft itself or the transmitting means themselves provide flexibility.  It is noted that:  The two groups of elements are thus related to different fields (see limiting and informative references below).

[F16C 32/00] Bearings not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details for all types of bearings, i.e. they are not limited to rotary sliding contact or rolling element bearings but, as far as applicable, they also cover the other bearing types (e.g. linear, magnetic, hydrostatic etc.).

[F16C 37/00] Cooling of bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means to remove heat from the bearing area, i.e. by radiation, conduction or heat exchange, excluding cooling lubricant or by means of lubricant (cf. groups F16C33/10 and F16C33/66 ).
[F16C 37/002] of fluid bearings
[F16C 37/005] of magnetic bearings
[F16C 37/007] of rolling bearings
[F16C 39/00] Relieving load on bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means exerting a force on the relative moving part directed against the load and cooperating in the load direction with the actual bearing which attains the desired position.  Means providing a preload on the bearing, which could be considered as negative relief, actually serve to reduce play and are e.g. to be classified in the appropriate groups (e.g. F16C25/00 , F16C29/12 , F16C32/067 ).
[F16C 39/02] using mechanical means
[F16C 39/04] using hydraulic or pneumatic means
[F16C 39/06] using magnetic means

[F16C 39/066] with opposing permanent magnets repelling each other
[F16C 41/00] Other accessories, -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elements which are not directly related to the bearing function, i.e. parts mounted to or integrated with the bearing but providing an additional functionality such as speed sensors, as far as there integration in the bearing is of relevance.  Load-equalizing elements.  Elements involved in protecting the bearing when not in use.
[F16C 41/001] Integrated brakes or clutches for stopping or coupling the relatively movable parts
[F16C 41/002] Conductive elements, e.g. to prevent static electricity

[F16C 41/04] Preventing damage to bearings during storage or transport thereof or when otherwise out of use
[F16C 41/045] Devices for provisionally retaining needles or rollers in a bearing race before mounting of the bearing on a shaft
[F16C 43/00] Assembling bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joining of the parts of a bearing, optionally simultaneously with mounting. It also embraces joining bearing parts which are split.  For a slide bearing this involves typically joining bearing bushes, shells and washers; optionally providing other parts such as seals.  For a rolling element bearing this involves typically providing rolling elements between the races, optionally providing other parts such as cages and seals.
[F16C 43/02] Assembling sliding-contact bearings
[F16C 43/04] Assembling rolling-contact bearings

[F16C 43/086] by plastic deformation of the race

[F16D] COUPLINGS FOR TRANSMITTING ROTATION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems of clutches or couplings for transmitting rotation used within a mechanism of general utility.  Combinations of clutches and brakes used within a mechanism of general utility.  Combinations couplings for transmitting rotation from one element to another and brakes used in conjunction with each other in a mechanism of general utility.  Combinations of couplings for transmitting rotation from one element to another and a clutch used in conjunction with each other in a mechanism of general utility.  Couplings per se for transmitting rotation of the following types:  Clutches per se of the following types:  Clutch components or special features related to clutches:  Brakes per se of the following types:  Brake components or special features related to brakes:  Friction linings in general:  Other types of couplings for transmitting rotation, clutches or brakes not provided for in another subclass of the CPC.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrodynamic brake systems for vehicles in general -> B60L7/00 - Vehicle brake control systems -> B60T - Conjoint control of vehicle sub-units of different type or different function, e.g. when at least one sub-unit is a clutch or a brake -> B60W - Fluid gearings -> F16H39/00 , F16H41/00 , F16H43/00 - Combinations of fluid gearings with clutches or couplings -> F16H45/00 - Combinations of mechanical gearings with fluid clutches -> F16H47/00 - Differential gearings, e.g. with freewheels or other clutches -> F16H48/00 - Dynamo-electric brakes or clutches, e.g. of the Eddy-current hysteresis type -> H02K49/00 - Clutches or holding devices using electrostatic attraction, e.g. using Johnson-Rahbek effect -> H02N13/00  **Glossary:** - actuator: a component of a mechanism that causes movement of a motion-conveying component or motion-inhibiting component but does not itself directly convey or inhibit motion. - brake: a mechanism with at least one component that is moved by an actuator to selectively or automatically engage or stop a member with respect to a stationary part. - clutch: a mechanism with at least two relatively movable components that are selectively or automatically engaged with each other by control means, e.g. an actuator, to transmit mechanical power or convey motion from a first member to a second member or disengaged by control means to stop transmitting mechanical power or conveying motion. - coupling for transmitting rotation: a mechanism that transmits torque or conveys rotary motion from a first shaft to a second shaft or other rotating member and that either does not include relatively moving components or, if it has relatively moving components, the components move without the use of control means.
[F16D 1/00] Couplings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Attachment of cranks to their shafts -> F16C3/10  **Glossary:** - Rigidly: "rigidly" means that there is no play or clearance intended in the connection (DE:spiel, FR:jeu).
[F16D 1/02] for connecting two abutting shafts or the like
[F16D 1/027] non-disconnectable, e.g. involving gluing, welding or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for adhesive connection or welding, but not limited thereto. Rivets are also non-disconnectable therefore this type of connection would also be classified here. But bolts are usually disconnectable and would be classified rather in F16D1/033 when connecting two faces perpendicular to the axis of rotation.
[F16D 1/033] by clamping together two faces perpendicular to the axis of rotation, e.g. with bolted flanges
[F16D 1/04] with clamping hub -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for connecting two abutting shafts or the like by means of a clamping hub or hub with a longitudinal key. Examples of clamping hubs (radial clamping/loading) and keys can be found in the titles of the subgroups of F16D1/08 .
[F16D 1/05] with radial clamping due to axial loading of at least one pair of conical surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for connecting two abutting shafts or the like by means of radial clamping due to axial loading of at least one pair of conical surfaces. Examples of this kind of clamping can be found in the subgroups of F16D1/09 .

[F16D 1/072] involving plastic deformation
[F16D 1/076] by clamping together two faces perpendicular to the axis of rotation, e.g. with bolted flanges
[F16D 1/08] with clamping hub -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a clamping hub or a hub with a longitudinal key.
[F16D 1/0805] with radial clamping due to deformation of a resilient body or a body of fluid  F16D1/091  takes precedence; elastic couplings  F16D3/80 ; fluid pressure clutches  F16D25/04 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to deformation of a resilient body or a body of fluid, e.g. used for hubs comprising cavities which can be pressurised by fluid, so that a wall of the hub can expand towards the shaft in order to radially clamp the shaft.
[F16D 1/0811] with radial clamping due to tilting of a hub part or ring about a diametral axis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to tilting of a hub part or ring about a diametral axis, meaning that radially clamping is achieved by means of tilting of a hub part or ring about an axis perpendicularly crossing the rotational axis.
[F16D 1/0817] with radial clamping due to rotation along an eccentric surface, e.g. arcuate wedging elements  similar clutches  F16D17/00 ; similar free-wheel clutches  F16D41/06 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to rotation along an eccentric surface, e.g. arcuate wedging elements on the inner surface of the hub and/or the mating outer surface of the shaft, whereby clamping is achieved when the shaft and hub are relatively rotated and the elements wedge.
[F16D 1/0823] with radial clamping of a helical wrap spring on the shaft or in the hub bore  similar clutches  F16D13/025 ,  F16D13/08 ,  F16D27/025 ,  F16D27/105 ; similar slip couplings  F16D7/022 ; similar free-wheel clutches  F16D41/206 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to clamping of a helical wrap spring on the shaft or in the hub bore, e.g. by expanding the wrap spring against the hub bore due to relative rotation of the spring ends around the rotational axis.

[F16D 1/0835] due to the elasticity of the ring or sleeve
[F16D 1/0841] due to axial loading of the ring or sleeve, e.g. Belleville washers
[F16D 1/0847] with radial clamping due to a radial screw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to clamping by a set screw, e.g. a screw screwed in the hub in a radial direction through the hub and ending in the hub bore and thereby hitting the shaft outer surface.
[F16D 1/0852] with radial clamping between the mating surfaces of the hub and shaft  F16D1/0805  -  F16D1/0817 ,  F16D1/09  take precedence
[F16D 1/0858] due to the elasticity of the hub (including shrink fits) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping between the mating surfaces of the hub and the shaft due to the elasticity of the hub, e.g. a hub is heated (to enlarge its bore), is then put on the shaft and subsequently cooled in order to crimp or e.g. a hub is press fit on a shaft.
[F16D 1/0864] due to tangential loading of the hub, e.g. a split hub -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping between the mating surfaces of the hub and the shaft due to tangential loading of the hub, e.g. a split hub, meaning e.g. a hub which has a radial split in the direction of the rotational axis, whereby the parts of the hub adjacent to the split and facing each other are clamped together by e.g. a screw so that the bore of the hub clamps around the shaft.

[F16D 2123/00] Multiple operation forces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This code indicates the presence of multiple independently operating actuators. When this code is assigned, each operation force is indexed additionally.

[F16D 3/223] the rolling members being guided in grooves in both coupling parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints in which flexibility is produced by means of rolling connecting parts, one coupling part entering a sleeve of the other coupling part and connected thereto by rolling members whereby the rolling members are being guided in grooves in both coupling parts.
[F16D 3/2233] where the track is made up of two curves with a point of inflexion in between, i.e. S-track joints -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves on both coupling parts. The projection of the groove lines forming a S curve with an inflection point, see inner contour of part (12) and outer contour of part (16) in the figure.
[F16D 3/2237] where the grooves are composed of radii and adjoining straight lines, i.e. undercut free [UF] type joints -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves on both coupling parts. In the projection these grooves are composed of radii and straight lines, see groove line (11b) and (12b) in the figure.
[F16D 3/224] the groove centre-lines in each coupling part lying on a sphere -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves in both coupling parts, and the groove centre-lines of each coupling part are lying on a sphere with the same centre.
[F16D 3/2245] where the groove centres are offset from the joint centre -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves in both coupling parts, the groove centre-lines are lying on spheres and the centres of these spheres (D1, G1) are offset from the joint centre (E1).  Each complete groove centre-line lying on (a part of) a single sphere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - In case the groove centre-lines are composed of combinations of parts of several spheres -> F16D3/223 .

[F16F] SPRINGS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Springs, shock-absorbers or vibration-dampers;  Their arrangement in, or adaptation for, particular apparatus if not provided for in the subclasses covering said apparatus.  **Glossary:** - Steel or metal: Mention of “steel” or “metal” in groups F16F , unless specific mention is made otherwise, should be seen in the light of the title of group F16F1/00 , i.e. material having low internal friction. This normally includes composite materials such as fibre-reinforced plastics. - Rubber or plastics: Mention of “rubber” or “plastics” in group F16F , unless specific mention is made otherwise, should be seen in the light of the title of group F16F1/36 , i.e. material having high internal friction. This normally does NOT include composite materials such as fibre-reinforced plastics except in the case of groups F16F1/366 - F16F1/3686 and F16F15/305 .

[F16G] BELTS, CABLES, OR ROPES, PREDOMINANTLY USED FOR DRIVING PURPOSES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Belts, e.g. V-belts, cables, ropes or chains that are specially adapted for transferring power/force from an input point to an output point to move or drive at least one mechanical part.  The belts, cables, ropes or chains being either  formed into a loop that is intended to travel around an endless path and engage two or more pulleys, sprockets or similar rotating elements and transfer power/force while travelling along its path; or  an elongated element that is intended to travel backward or forward along a path to transfer power/force between one point along its length to another point along its length, e.g. from a drum to a load.  Chains, e.g. hauling- or hoisting-chains, that are not specially adapted for transferring power/force.  Fittings and attachments primarily intended to be connected to or used with the above types of belts, cables, ropes or chains. These include:  means for fastening ends of belts together to form closed loops, e.g. fastenings for conveyor belts or V-belts;  means for fastening cables or ropes to one another or to other objects;  caps or sleeves for fixing on cables or ropes;  means for coupling chains;  components of chains, e.g. shackles, swivels, chain joints, chain links or chain bushes;  hooks as integral parts of chains.
[F16G 1/00] Driving-belts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Belts adapted for transferring power/force from an input point to an output point to move or drive at least one mechanical part. [F16H] GEARING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings for conveying rotary motion:  Combination of gearings.  General details of gearings.  Control of gearings.  **Glossary:** - gearing: mechanical, hydraulic, electric or other means for transmitting mechanical motion or force - gearbox: housing of the gearing - toothed gearing: includes worm gearing and other gearing involving at least one wheel or sector provided with teeth or the equivalent, except gearing with chains or toothed belts, which is treated as friction gearing - conveying motion: includes transmitting energy, and means that the applied and resultant motions are of the same kind, though they may differ in, e.g. speed, direction or extent - rotary motion: implies that the motion may continue indefinitely - oscillating motion: moving about an axis to an extent which is limited by the construction of the gearing and which may exceed one revolution, the movement being alternately forwards and backwards during continued operation of the gearing - reciprocating motion: moving substantially in a straight line, the movement being alternately forwards and backwards during continued operation of the gearing - reversing or reversal: applied movement in one direction may produce a resultant movement in either of two opposed directions at will. Note: When reversing reciprocating motion, input rotary motion (which is defined as indefinitely continuous rotary motion) would cause an automatic reversal of the reciprocating motion. If the input rotational direction is changed in order to cause reversal of the reciprocating motion, the input motion is an oscillating motion (which is defined as alternately forward and backward rotary motion) - central gears: includes any gears whose axis is the main axis of the gearing, e.g. sun or ring gear - Ravigneaux set: a planetary gear set with at least three central gears, and conveying rotary motion between axially-spaced orbital gears. It comprises a long orbital gear consisting of two axially spaced orbital gears which are fixedly connected to each other. It comprises a further orbital gear which meshes with one of the orbital gears of the long orbital gear to form a pair of intermeshing orbital gears. All orbital gears are mounted on a common planet carrier and are considered as a single set of orbital gears. - creeping: the vehicle has come to a stop, the engine is at idle (i.e., there is no request by the operator for a higher engine speed/torque), but due to the rotation of various transmission components, the vehicle starts to move, and the vehicle operator has some control over movement with a brake - inching: the vehicle operator has some control, besides using a brake, over moving the vehicle by small degrees
[F16H 1/00] Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearing with fixed gear ratio using only gears with teeth.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gearing for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio of for reversing rotary motion -> F16H3/00
[F16H 1/003] Unidirectionally torque-transmitting toothed gearing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion in which torque may be transmitted from the input to the output in only one input rotation direction, e.g. a clockwise input rotation is possible whereas a counter-clockwise input rotation is blocked.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates an example in which clockwise rotation is possible.  1b.  Figure 1b illustrates an example in which counter clockwise rotation is blocked.  2a.  2b.  Figures 2a and 2b illustrate an example in which rotation is allowed in only one direction.

Figure 2 illustrates a drive shaft (left outermost gear) and a driven shaft (right outermost gear) may assume variable positions to one another, i.e. gear train (252B) may pivot with respect to gear train (252A).

[F16H 1/28] with gears having orbital motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion. In addition, due to rotation of the planet carrier (7), planet gears (5) also orbit relative to and around rotating sun gear (4), i.e. they have orbital motion.

[F16H 2001/2872] comprising three central gears, i.e. ring or sun gear, engaged by at least one common orbital gear mounted on an idling carrier -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Planetary gearing having a fixed gear ratio and including three central gears which are all engaged with the same planet gear, and the planet gear is mounted on an idling carrier.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a planetary gearing with a fixed gear ratio and including three central gears, i.e. sun gear (2) and two ring gears (3) and (4), which are all engaged by a common orbital gear (6) mounted on carrier (5). Carrier (5) is not connected to any shaft and therefore idles. In other words, carrier (5) constitutes an "idling carrier".

[F16H 2003/0807] with gear ratios in which the power is transferred by axially coupling idle gears to each other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates an eight-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion, comprising idle gears (5) and (6) which are axially couplable to each other by shift element (I), which changes the gear ratio.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion, including idle gears (33) and (34) which may be axially coupled to each other by engaging clutch (38), in order to transfer power.  **Glossary:** - winding transmission: a transmission including a gear ratio that is established by using multiple gear pairs in the transmission. For example, a transmission including a 1st gear ratio achieved by using more than one of the other existing gear pairs. In this 1 st gear ratio, torque is wound through the transmission via the several gear pairs. Thereby, a separate gear plane for the 1st gear ratio is not necessary. This concept is often applied to the 1st gear and the reverse gear. These gears are usually used only during short periods, such that the reduced efficiency by using several gear pairs instead of only one gear pair can be neglected. - winding gear ratio: a speed, e.g. 1 st speed, which is achieved by winding torque through a winding transmission

It uses unsynchronised clutches (9), (11) and (12).

[F16H 2035/005] Gearings or mechanisms preventing back-driving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing with a no-back mechanism consisting of an axially movable friction member (10). If the torque exceeds a certain threshold, breaking portions (24) and (25) break such that torque transfer is interrupted and overload is prevented.

[F16H 2037/023] the combined gearing being provided with at least two forward and one reverse ratio in a serially arranged sub-transmission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of mechanical gearings comprising essentially only toothed gearing combined with continuously variable friction gearing [CVT]. The combination of gearing includes a serially arranged sub-transmission with at least two forward ratios and one reverse ratio.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a combination of a combination of mechanical gearings including a belt-type CVT 2 and a toothed sub-transmission (29) in series. Transmission (29) includes a reverse clutch (25), a brake (26) for a low forward speed and a clutch (28) for a high forward speed. Thus, the transmission (29) provides two forward ratios and one reverse ratio.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a combination of mechanical gearings including belt-type CVT (121) and a toothed sub-transmission (140) in series between input gear (120) and output gear (152). Transmission (140) includes a low forward speed, a high forward speed and a reverse speed. Thus, the transmission (140) provides two forward ratios and one reverse ratio.
[F16H 2037/025] having continuously variable friction gearing, i.e. CVT, in which the ratio coverage is used more than once to produce the overall transmission ratio coverage, e.g. by shift to end of range, then change ratio in sub-transmission and shift CVT through range once again -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of mechanical gearings comprising essentially only toothed gearing combined with continuously variable friction gearing [CVT]. The CVT includes a ratio coverage that is used more than once to produce the overall transmission ratio coverage, e.g. by shift to end of range, then change ratio in sub-transmission and shift CVT through range once again.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates a transmission comprising a combination of mechanical gearings including a belt-type CVT (H) and toothed gearing (Z1), (Z2), (Z3), (Z4), (Z5) and (Z6). The transmission ratio of CVT (H) is (i S ), and the transmission ratio of the whole transmission (the combined gearing) is (i G ). The CVT ratio (i S ) is shifted through its range from 0.5 to 2, then the toothed gearing is shifted at whole transmission ratio (i G ) of 2. Thereafter, the CVT ratio (i S ) is shifted through its range in the other direction, i.e. from 2 to 0.5. In other words, the CVT (H) is shifted through its range, the toothed gearing is then shifted, and the CVT (H) is shifted through its range again.  1b.

Forward-reverse output unit (40) includes reversing gear (42).  2. [F16H 2037/088] Power-split transmissions with summing differentials, with the input of the CVT connected or connectable to the input shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Power-split transmissions including summing planetary gearing and with a CVT in at least one torque path. [F16H 2037/0893] characterised in that the ratio of the continuously variable transmission is different from zero when the output shaft speed is zero -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Power-split transmissions including summing or distributing planetary gearing and with a CVT in at least one torque path. The transmission includes a speed ratio where the output shaft speed is zero while the individual CVT ratio is different from zero, i.e. a "geared neutral" speed ratio.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates a power-split transmission including a combination of a toroidal-type CVT (8), (9) and planetary or differential gearing (16), (30). The overall ratio of the transmission may be changed from reverse speed ratio R through zero speed ratio N to forward speed ratio FL merely by changing the ratio of the CVT. At speed ratio N, the speed of output shaft (40) is zero while the speed of input shaft (1) is different from zero and the ratio of the toroidal CVT (8) and (9) is different from zero. The speed ratio N is otherwise known as "geared neutral" since this results in zero rotation speed of the output shaft while the input shaft is rotating.  1b.  **Glossary:** - geared neutral: input shaft speed is different from zero though output shaft speed is zero. It is noted that this enables drive-off without using a torque converter or disconnecting clutch.

[F16H 21/16] for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion
[F16H 21/18] Crank gearings
[F16H 21/20] with adjustment of throw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only links or levers, with or without slides, crank gearings or eccentric gearings, for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion, all movement being in, or parallel to, a single plane, with adjustment of throw.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links and levers for converting rotary motion of the crank shaft (1) into reciprocating motion of the piston (13) which functions as a guided slide. Crankshaft (1) comprises an oblique crank pin (9). The throw, i.e. the length of the reciprocating motion of piston (13), is adjusted by moving crankshaft (1) in a different axial position. The axial movement of crankshaft (1) between a minimum and maximum throw is shown by arrow (7).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links and levers for converting rotary motion of the crank shaft (18) into reciprocating motion of the piston (13) which functions as a guide slide. The throw, i.e. the length of the reciprocating motion of piston (13), is adjusted by moving pivot (12) to a different position.
[F16H 21/22] with one connecting-rod and one guided slide to each crank or eccentric
[F16H 21/24] without further links or guides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a crank gearing comprising primarily only links or levers for converting rotary motion of crank shaft (21) into reciprocating motion of piston (14) which functions as a guided slide via connecting rod (7). The crank gearing does not comprise any further links or guides in addition to the one connecting rod (7) and one guided slide (14).
[F16H 21/26] with toggle action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Crank gearings or eccentric gearings comprising primarily only links or levers, for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion. All movement is in, or parallel to, a single plane, with one connecting-rod and one guided slide to each crank or eccentric. The gearing includes joints or levers which may toggle, or move, between two locations.
[F16H 21/28] with cams or additional guides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links or levers for converting rotary motion of crank shaft (11) into reciprocating motion of piston (1) which functions as a guided slide. Crank pin (8) moves along groove (6), such that groove (6) functions as a cam. Connecting rod (7) is guided by crosshead (12) functioning as an additional guide.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links or levers for converting rotary motion of the crank shaft into reciprocating motion of the piston which functions as a guided slide. The gearing further includes a crosshead a functioning as an additional guide.  3a.  3b.  Figures 3a and 3b illustrate a crank gearing comprising primarily only links or levers for converting rotary motion of crank shaft (k) into reciprocating motion of piston (b) which functions as a guided slide. Pin (d) at the lower end of connecting rod (c) interacts with a cam (g) as well as a long hole (h) which also functions as a cam of crank arm (i).
